lesson:
十じゅう (ten) + 分ぶん (parts). All ten parts — a full set, nothing missing. When you have 十分じゅうぶん of something, you have enough. 十分じゅうぶんです (that's enough / that's plenty). 十分じゅうぶんな時間じかん (sufficient time). 十じゅう分わに気をつけて (be fully careful).

Watch out: 十分じゅうぶん read as じゅっぷん means "ten minutes." Same kanji, different reading, totally different word. Context always disambiguates, but in writing you'll occasionally do a double-take. 十じゅう分わ待まってください could be "please wait ten minutes" (じゅっぷん) or "please wait sufficiently" (じゅうぶん) — but in practice, the "ten minutes" reading is overwhelmingly more likely in that sentence.

As a な-adjective: 十じゅう分わな準備じゅんび (sufficient preparation). As an adverb: 十じゅう分わ分かっている (I fully understand). The word carries a tone of reassurance — "enough, don't worry about it." もう十じゅう分わです is a polite way to decline more food, more help, more anything.

Dictionaryじゅうぶんだ
Negativeじゅうぶんじゃない
Pastじゅうぶんだった
Past negじゅうぶんじゃなかった
Adverbじゅうぶんに
て-formじゅうぶんで

meaning: not much, not really, not particularly, not so
lesson:
あまり almost always appears with a negative. あまり好すきじゃない (I don't really like it). あまり食たべなかった (I didn't eat much). あまり分わからない (I don't really understand). The pattern is simple: あまり + negative = "not very" / "not much."

What makes it useful is the softness. 好すきじゃない alone is a flat "I don't like it." Add あまり and it becomes "I don't particularly like it" — politely lukewarm rather than bluntly negative. Japanese conversation relies heavily on this kind of hedging. あまり gives you a way to express mild negation without sounding harsh.

There's a rarer positive usage that means "too much" or "excessively": あまりの暑あつさに (because of the excessive heat). あまりにもひどい (too terrible). In this sense, the word has a completely different flavor — not mild but extreme. You'll encounter it in writing and dramatic speech, but the negative-pairing usage is what you'll need daily.

写しゃ (copy, reproduce) + 真しん (truth, reality). A copy of truth. A reproduction of reality. When this compound was coined in the 19th century, photography was literal magic — a machine that could copy the real world. The name captures that wonder: not a painting, not an interpretation, but truth itself, reproduced.

写真しゃしんを撮とる (to take a photo) uses 撮る (とる), the specific "take" for photography and film. 写真しゃしんを見みせて (show me the photo). In the smartphone era, 写真しゃしん is everywhere: 写真しゃしん撮ってもいいですか (may I take a photo?) is an essential phrase for travel in Japan.

The kanji 真しん deserves a closer look. Its original meaning is "genuine, real" — it shows up in 真しん実じつ (しんじつ, truth), 真しん面めん目め (まじめ, serious/earnest), and 真しんん中なか (まんなか, the very center). Whenever you see 真しん in a word, it's adding emphasis on authenticity — the real deal.

繰く (reel, wind thread) + 返かえす (to return). Winding the thread back and doing it again. The image of a loom shuttle going back and forth — the same motion, over and over. 同おなじミスを繰くり返かえす (repeat the same mistake). 歴史れきしは繰くり返かえす (history repeats itself).

The word often carries a note of frustration or inevitability. When something is 繰くり返かえされる (repeated), it's usually not a celebration — it's a complaint about things not improving, or an observation about cycles. 何なに度も繰り返かえした (did it over and over) has a dogged, weary quality. Compare with もう一いち回かい (one more time), which is neutral, or また (again), which is lighter.

That said, it's perfectly functional in neutral contexts: もう一いち度繰り返かえしてください (please repeat that one more time) is a polite request in class or conversation. The nuance depends on context, not the word itself.

Particles: 繰くり返かえす takes を — the thing repeated is the direct object. 同じおなじ言い葉を繰り返かえす (repeat the same words).

Potential が繰くり返かえせる: が — what can be repeated. 成功せいこうが繰り返かえせるとは限らない (you can't necessarily repeat success).

Passive に繰くり返かえされる: に — used when something keeps being repeated (often impersonal). 同じおなじ過ちが繰くり返かえされている (the same mistake keeps being repeated).

Causative に…を繰くり返かえさせる: に marks who you make repeat, を marks what. 生う徒にフレーズを繰り返かえさせる (make students repeat the phrase).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryくりかえすを
Negativeくりかえさないを
Politeくりかえしますを
Polite negくりかえしませんを
Pastくりかえしたを
Polite pastくりかえしましたを
て-formくりかえしてを
Volitionalくりかえそうを
Polite volくりかえしましょうを
Passiveくりかえされるに
Causativeくりかえさせるに／を
Caus-passくりかえさせられるに
Potentialくりかえせるが
Imperativeくりかえせを
Imp なさいくりかえしなさいを
Cond ～ばくりかえせばを
Cond ～たらくりかえしたらを

種しゅ (seed, species) + 類るい (kind, type, class). Seeds sorted by type. 種しゅ originally depicted grains of rice being planted — each variety a different seed. 類るい has the rice radical too, suggesting different grains being classified. Two kanji about sorting things into groups.

何なに種類？ (how many types?) is the essential question. いろいろな種類るいしゅるいがある (there are various types). 三さん種類の魚さかな (three types of fish). The word works as a counter-like noun — you put a number in front of it to specify how many kinds.

種類 vs タイプ: The loanword タイプ is used for personal preferences and personalities — 彼かれは私わたしのタイプじゃない (he's not my type). 種類るいしゅるい is for actual categorization of things — species, varieties, classifications. You wouldn't say 彼は私の種類じゃない. Different domains.

意い (mind, intention) + 見けん (see, view). Your mind's view — what you see when you look at an issue from inside your own head. An opinion.

意い見みを言いう (state one's opinion). 意い見みが違う (our opinions differ). ご意い見みをお聞きかせください (please let us hear your opinion — formal). The word is neutral in register and fits everywhere from casual discussion to business meetings.

As a する-noun, 意い見みする means "to give someone a piece of your mind" — to admonish or criticize. This is a different flavor from simply "having" an opinion: 部へ下したに意見みする (admonish a subordinate). The する-verb usage carries authority — you're not sharing your view, you're correcting someone. It's mildly old-fashioned but still appears in formal contexts.

新しん (new) + 聞ぶん (hear, listen). New things heard — news. The same etymological logic as English "newspaper" (new + paper) but from the ear rather than the page. Chinese uses the same compound, and it was borrowed into Japanese during the Meiji era when newspapers arrived as a Western import.

聞ぶん here uses its on'yomi ぶん, not the kun'yomi きく you know from the verb. This is the compound reading — the formal, Chinese-origin pronunciation. 聞ぶん in this sense means "things heard, reports" — it lives in 新聞きしんぶん社 (しんぶんしゃ, newspaper company) and 新聞きしんぶん記者 (しんぶんきしゃ, newspaper reporter).

新聞しんぶんを読よむ (read the newspaper). 新聞きしんぶんに載っていた (it was in the newspaper). 朝あさ刊 (ちょうかん, morning edition) and 夕ゆう刊 (ゆうかん, evening edition) are the two daily deliveries in Japan's still-robust newspaper culture. Physical newspaper delivery to homes remains far more common in Japan than in most Western countries.

meaning: sentence(s), writing (in general), any written text
lesson:
文ぶん (sentence, writing, pattern) + 章しょう (chapter, badge, section). Writing organized into sections — a piece of text, whether it's a single sentence, a paragraph, an essay, or a full document. 文章ぶんしょう is the go-to word when you're talking about written text as a body of work.

文 vs 文章: 文ぶん (ぶん) alone means a single sentence. 文章ぶんしょう means a connected piece of writing — multiple sentences forming something coherent. この文は長ながい (this sentence is long). この文章ぶんしょうは分わかりやすい (this writing is easy to understand). The distinction matters when talking about language study: grammar drills deal in 文ぶん; reading comprehension deals in 文章ぶんしょう.

文章ぶんしょうを書かく (write a text). 文章ぶんしょう力ちから (ぶんしょうりょく, writing ability). 文章ぶんしょうが上うえ手て (good at writing). The word shows up often in school and work contexts — Japanese education places heavy emphasis on 文章ぶんしょう composition.

目め (eye) + 立たつ (to stand). Something that stands up to the eye — it catches your attention, it's conspicuous. The compound is beautifully literal: the thing rises from the background and your eyes lock onto it.

The word is neutral — standing out can be good or bad. 目立めだつ色いろ (a conspicuous color). 彼女かのじょはクラスで目立めだつ (she stands out in class) — could be talent, beauty, or disruptive behavior. ミスが目め立たつ (mistakes stand out / errors are noticeable) — that's bad. 目立めだたないようにする (try not to stand out) — sometimes the Japanese priority.

That last usage touches on something cultural. Japan has the famous proverb 出でる杭は打たれる (the nail that sticks out gets hammered down). 目立めだつ is that nail. Depending on the context, conspicuousness is admired or avoided — and the word itself carries both possibilities with equal ease.

Particles: 目立めだつ takes が — the conspicuous thing is the subject. 傷きずが目め立たつ (the scratch is noticeable). No direct object — this is an intransitive verb.

Passive: rare, since the verb is intransitive. Indirect passive is possible but uncommon.

Causative を目め立たたせる: making something stand out. ポイントを目め立たたせる (make the key point stand out). This causative form is actually quite common in design and presentation contexts.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryめだつが
Negativeめだたないが
Politeめだちますが
Polite negめだちませんが
Pastめだったが
Polite pastめだちましたが
て-formめだってが
Volitionalめだとうが
Polite volめだちましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばめだてばが
Cond ～たらめだったらが

相あい (mutual, together) + 手て (hand, person). The hand on the other side — the person facing you. But 相手あいて is deliberately vague about the relationship. It could be your opponent, your partner, your conversation counterpart, or simply "the other person involved." The word refuses to specify whether the relationship is friendly or hostile.

相あい手てチーム (the opposing team). 結婚けっこん相手て (marriage partner). 話はなし相手て (someone to talk to — a conversation partner). 相あい手てにしない (to ignore someone — literally, not make them your counterpart). Same word, wildly different contexts.

The real utility of 相手あいて is that it lets you talk about "the other person" without naming them or specifying the dynamic. English doesn't have a clean equivalent — we'd say "opponent" in sports, "partner" in business, "the other person" in dating. Japanese just uses 相手あいて for all of them and lets context do the work.

病びょう (illness) + 院いん (institution, hall). An illness institution. The kanji 病びょう has 疒やまいだれ (the sickness radical — a person collapsed on a bed) with 丙 inside contributing the sound. That sickness radical 疒やまいだれ appears in every disease-related kanji: 痛いた (pain), 疲つか (fatigue), 症しょう (symptom). Spot the little bed and you know someone's unwell.

In Japan, 病院びょういん covers both hospitals and what English speakers would call "clinics" or "doctor's offices." There's no strong linguistic distinction between a huge medical center and a small neighborhood practice — both get called 病院びょういん in casual speech. For precision, a large hospital is 総合あ病院びょういん (そうごうびょういん, general hospital) and a small clinic is クリニック or 医院 (いいん).

病院びょういんに行いく (go to the hospital) doesn't carry the alarming connotation it does in English. In Japanese, it just means "go see a doctor" — for a cold, a checkup, anything.

Four words pronounced あつい. Four different kanji. This is a minefield. 厚あつい = thick (a thick book, thick fabric). 熱あつい = hot to touch (hot coffee). 暑あつい = hot weather (a summer day). 篤あつい = devoted, serious (rare, literary). In speech they're identical. In writing, context and kanji sort them out.

厚あつい specifically is about physical thickness or metaphorical depth. 厚あつい本ほん (a thick book). 厚あつい雲くも (thick clouds). 面めんの皮が厚あつい (thick-skinned — shameless). 厚あついもてなし (warm/generous hospitality — "thick" welcome). The metaphorical uses lean toward abundance and generosity.

Its antonym is 薄うすい (うすい, thin), which you'll see soon. Keep them as a pair: 厚あつい / 薄うすい, thick / thin.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

The kanji 忙ぼう is 忄りっしんべん (heart radical) + 亡ぼう (lost, dead, gone). A lost heart. When you're busy, your heart — your presence of mind — goes missing. You're so occupied that the essential "you" has checked out. It's a remarkably honest etymology for what busyness actually feels like.

最近さいきん忙いそがしい (I've been busy lately) might be the most frequently uttered sentence in Japanese working life. The word does double duty: it describes both the objective state (having many things to do) and the subjective feeling (being overwhelmed). 忙いそがしくて行いけない (I'm too busy to go) is a perfectly acceptable excuse for declining invitations — perhaps the safest one, since busyness in Japan is quasi-respectable.

Sound anchor: i-so-ga-shii has four syllables that tumble out in a rush, mirroring the feeling. The long しい ending makes it whiny, almost like a complaint. Which it often is.

Dictionaryいそがしい
Negativeいそがしくない
Pastいそがしかった
Past negいそがしくなかった
Adverbいそがしく
て-formいそがしくて

薄うすい is the opposite of 厚あつい for physical thickness, but it does much more. It covers four English adjectives in one: thin (paper, walls), weak (tea, flavor), light (color, makeup), and pale (skin, hope). The common thread: not enough substance. Something that should be denser, stronger, or deeper — but isn't.

薄うすい壁かべ (thin walls). コーヒーが薄うすい (the coffee is weak). 薄うすい色いろ (a pale/light color). 印象が薄うすい (the impression is faint — unmemorable). Each usage maps to a different English word, but Japanese sees them all as the same quality: insufficient density.

The kanji 薄はく has the grass radical (艹くさかんむり) on top — suggesting something spread thin, like grass covering a wide area without much depth. The component 溥 underneath contributes both sound and meaning (to spread thinly). A useful mnemonic: grass is wide but never thick.

Dictionaryうすい
Negativeうすくない
Pastうすかった
Past negうすくなかった
Adverbうすく
て-formうすくて

Three vertical strokes. That's the whole kanji. 川かわ is a picture of water flowing between two banks — the oldest, most transparent pictograph still in daily use. Oracle bone script from 3,000+ years ago shows three wavy vertical lines; modern 川かわ just straightened them out. Look at it: the left and right lines are the riverbanks. The middle is the current.

川かわ vs 河かわ: both mean "river," but 川かわ (かわ) is the native Japanese word for any river, while 河かわ (usually かわ or が) shows up in formal or literary contexts and in the names of specifically large rivers. In practice, 川かわ is the one you'll use. It appears in countless place names — 多おお摩川かわ (Tama River), 川かわ崎 (Kawasaki — "river cape"), 石いし川かわ (Ishikawa — "stone river").

The word doubles as a metaphor for time and inevitability: 時ときの流れ (the flow of time) draws on the same image. A river can't flow backward. In haiku and Japanese poetry, 川かわ is never just water — it's impermanence.

暗くらい covers physical darkness and emotional gloom in one word. A dark room and a dark personality use the same adjective. 部屋へやが暗くらい (the room is dark). 暗くらい顔かお (a gloomy face). 暗くらい話はなし (a depressing story). The physical and emotional meanings bleed into each other effortlessly — darkness is sadness, in any language.

Its opposite is 明あかるい (あかるい, bright/cheerful). The pair 暗くらい / 明あかるい maps onto English dark/bright for both rooms and moods.

The kanji 暗あん is 日にち (sun) + 音おん (sound). Sun plus sound seems odd for "dark," but the 音おん component is there for the sound value, not the meaning. A different theory: the sun is hidden, and all you have left is sound — you're in the dark, navigating by ear. Whether that's the real etymology or a convenient mnemonic, it sticks. When you can't see, you listen.

There's also a usage meaning "unfamiliar with": その分野ぶんやには暗くらい (I'm in the dark about that field — I don't know much about it). This mirrors the English idiom perfectly.

Dictionaryくらい
Negativeくらくない
Pastくらかった
Past negくらくなかった
Adverbくらく
て-formくらくて

From English "class," but narrower. クラス in Japanese primarily means a homeroom class — the group of students you're assigned to, who take all their classes together and share a 教室きょうしつ (classroom). In Japanese schools, it's the students who move between rooms for different subjects (in middle and high school) or stay put while teachers rotate in (elementary). Your クラス is your social unit for the year.

クラスメート (classmate). クラスの雰囲気ふんいき (the atmosphere of the class). 同じおなじクラスになった (we ended up in the same class). In Japanese school life, being in the same クラス as your friend is a big deal — it determines your daily social world for an entire year.

Outside schools, クラス can mean "class" in the sense of grade or rank: ビジネスクラス (business class), ファーストクラス (first class). But for a course or lesson, Japanese uses 授業じゅぎょう (じゅぎょう) or レッスン, not クラス.

The kanji 黒くろ has fire (灬れっか, the four-dot fire radical at the bottom) and a blocked-off space above it. The original meaning: the color left by fire — soot, char, the blackness that flames leave behind. Every other color kanji in Japanese has a different origin, but 黒くろ is born from burning.

黒くろい猫ねこ (a black cat). 黒くろい服ふく (black clothes). 黒くろい雲くも (dark clouds). The adjective form 黒くろい goes directly before nouns. The noun form 黒くろ (くろ) is the color itself — used when referring to black as a concept or choice: 黒くろにする (I'll go with black).

In figurative usage, 黒くろ means guilty or shady — the opposite of 白しろ (innocent, clean). あの人ひとは黒くろだ (that person is guilty / shady). 黒くろ幕 (くろまく, the person pulling strings behind the scenes — literally "black curtain"). Japanese noir, built right into the language.

Dictionaryくろい
Negativeくろくない
Pastくろかった
Past negくろくなかった
Adverbくろく
て-formくろくて

From English "bus," itself shortened from Latin "omnibus" (for everyone). The journey from Latin to English to Japanese stripped it down to two syllables: ba-su. Japan's bus system is extensive and reliable, especially in areas where trains don't reach.

バスに乗のる (ride the bus — に marks what you board). バスを降おりる (get off the bus — を marks what you step out of). バス停 (ばすてい, bus stop). バスが来くた (the bus is here). The particle switch between に (boarding) and を (alighting) trips up learners — remember: に is where you enter, を is what you leave.

Watch out: バス can also mean "bass" (as in bass guitar or a bass voice), and in older usage, "bath" (from English "bath" — though 風呂ふろ is far more common for bathing). Context makes the distinction instant, but don't be startled if you see バス in a music context.

青あおい is the color that broke English speakers' brains. It means "blue" — sky, ocean, blue jeans. But it also means "green" — traffic lights, unripe fruit, fresh vegetables. The 青あお traffic signal in Japan is officially called 青あお even though it looks green. The 青あお apple is unripe even though unripe apples are green. How?

Old Japanese had fewer color categories than modern English. 青あお originally covered the entire blue-green spectrum — everything from deep navy to fresh leaf green. Over time, 緑みどり (みどり) split off to handle green specifically, but the old 青あお territory never fully retreated. Traffic lights, green apples, and lush foliage kept their 青あお label. It's not that Japanese people can't see the difference between blue and green — it's that the language drew the color boundary in a different place than English did.

青あおい空そら (blue sky). 顔かおが青あおい (pale-faced — drained of color, like turning blue/white). 青春せいしゅん (せいしゅん, youth — literally "blue spring," the green/fresh season of life). That last compound captures the 青あお worldview perfectly: young, raw, unripe, full of potential. Not quite a color. More like a stage of being.

Dictionaryあおい
Negativeあおくない
Pastあおかった
Past negあおくなかった
Adverbあおく
て-formあおくて

The kanji 薬くすり has the grass radical 艹くさかんむり on top — because medicine came from plants. Underneath sits 楽がく, which means "comfortable" or "pleasant." Grass that makes you comfortable: medicine. The etymology is so clean it barely needs explaining, and that's exactly what makes it stick.

くすり covers both "medicine" and "drug" with no distinction. 薬くすりを飲のむ (take medicine — literally "drink" it, even pills). 薬くすりが効く (the medicine works/takes effect). 薬くすり屋や (くすりや, pharmacy/drugstore). The word is neutral — context tells you whether it's Tylenol or something illicit.

Notice the verb pairing: you 飲のむ medicine in Japanese, not "eat" it and not "take" it. Even a cream you rub on your skin gets described differently — 薬くすりを塗る (ぬる, to apply/spread). But anything that goes in your mouth is 飲のむ, regardless of whether it's liquid or solid.

Sound first: sa-tou sounds almost exactly like the English name "Sato" — one of the most common Japanese surnames. If you know anyone named Sato, picture them made of sugar. Ridiculous, but that's the point.

The kanji tell a material story. 砂さ means sand — granular, dry, pourable. 糖とう means sugar as a substance (the chemistry kind). Together: sand-sugar, granulated sweetness. 糖とう has the rice radical 米こめ on the left, because sugar was historically processed from grain starches in East Asia before cane sugar arrived. The right side 唐 (Tang dynasty China) provides the sound and a hint at the trade route that brought refined sugar techniques eastward.

砂糖さとう is specifically granulated sugar — the white stuff. For the broader concept of sweetness or sugar as a nutrient, Japanese uses 糖とう alone in compounds: 糖とう分わ (とうぶん, sugar content), 血糖とう (けっとう, blood sugar).

休やすみ is the noun form of 休やすむ (to rest), and it covers an enormous range of English words: a five-minute break, a day off, a national holiday, summer vacation. 今日にちきょうは休やすみです could mean "I have the day off" or "we're closed today" or "it's a holiday." The word doesn't care about the scale — if activity stops, that's 休やすみ.

This flexibility matters practically. 夏休なつやすみ (なつやすみ, summer break). 昼ひる休やすみ (ひるやすみ, lunch break). お休やすみ (おやすみ, goodnight — literally "honorable rest"). That last one is worth noting: お休やすみなさい is the standard way to say goodnight, and it's built right from this word. You're wishing someone a good rest.

The kanji 休やす is a person (亻にんべん) leaning against a tree (木き). A worker resting in the shade. It's one of those kanji pictures that actually works as a mnemonic without any stretching.

Three-kanji compound, and each piece does clear work. 郵ゆう means postal/mail. 便びん means convenience or delivery — the same 便びん in 便利 (べんり, convenient). 局きょく means bureau or office — a government or institutional location. So: mail-delivery-bureau. Bureaucratic precision in three characters.

局きょく is the useful piece to pull out. It shows up in any institutional office: 薬局やっきょく (やっきょく, pharmacy), 放送おく局 (ほうそうきょく, broadcasting station), 電話はなでんわ局 (でんわきょく, telephone office). When you see 〜局きょく, think "official place where X happens."

Japan's post offices double as banks — you can open a savings account, send money, buy insurance, all at the 郵便局ゆうびんきょく. The postal banking system (ゆうちょ銀行いぎんこう) is one of the largest financial institutions in the world. So 郵便局ゆうびんきょく in daily Japanese life is closer to "post office slash bank" than the stripped-down stamp-and-package operation most Westerners picture.

住じゅう (to live, reside) + 所しょ (place) = the place where you live. Your address. Clean logic, easy to remember.

But here's what makes Japanese addresses disorienting for Westerners: they don't work like street addresses. There are no street names in most of Japan. Instead, addresses drill down by area: prefecture → city → ward → district → block → building number. 東ひがし京都渋谷区神宮前まえ1-2-3 doesn't name a street at all — it names nested zones, narrowing from broad to specific. Your 住所じゅうしょ is a set of coordinates in a grid of administrative boundaries, not a position along a named road.

This means that when someone asks 住所じゅうしょを教おしえてください (please tell me your address), the answer is a chain of numbers and area names that might not help you navigate at all without a map. That's why Japanese people so commonly draw little maps when giving directions — the 住所じゅうしょ tells the post office where to deliver, but it doesn't tell a visitor how to walk there.

こちら is the polite version of こっち — both mean "this way" or "here" (my side). You already know the ko-so-a-do system from これ・それ・あれ・どれ. This is the same system in a different outfit: こちら・そちら・あちら・どちら. The 〜ちら series adds politeness and a sense of direction — not just "this thing" but "this side, this direction, over here."

In practice, こちら does double duty as a polite way to introduce someone or present yourself. A receptionist gesturing toward a room: こちらへどうぞ (this way, please). Introducing a colleague: こちらは田中たなかさんです (this is Mr. Tanaka). On the phone, identifying yourself: こちらは山田やまだです (this is Yamada — literally "this side is Yamada").

The pattern is tidy: こっち for friends, こちら for everyone else. Same meaning, different register. You'll hear こちら constantly in shops, restaurants, and offices — it's the default polite spatial word.

財ざい means wealth (there's 貝かい, the shell-money radical, on the right). 布 means cloth. A wealth-cloth — a piece of fabric you keep your money in. Wallets were literally pouches once, and the kanji remembers that.

さいふ is gender-neutral in Japanese — it covers wallets, billfolds, coin purses, whatever holds your money. 財布さいふを忘わすれた (I forgot my wallet) is one of those sentences you'll unfortunately need. 財布さいふを落おとした (I dropped/lost my wallet) is the other one.

Japan is still heavily cash-based compared to most developed countries, which makes the 財布さいふ a more central daily object than it might be elsewhere. Losing yours is a genuine crisis. The good news: Japan's lost-and-found culture is legendary. A wallet dropped on a Tokyo street has a remarkably high chance of being turned in intact, cash and all.

Straight from English: "passport" → パスポート. The katakana maps the English sounds as faithfully as the Japanese phonetic system allows — pa-su-po-o-to. If you can say "passport" slowly, you can say this word.

Loanwords like this are freebies for English speakers — you get hundreds of vocabulary words essentially for free. The catch is pronunciation. English speakers tend to say these words with English rhythm and stress, which makes them unintelligible to Japanese listeners. The trick: flatten everything. No stress on any syllable. Each mora gets equal time. PA-SU-PO-O-TO, five even beats. Practice that flatness and every katakana loanword in your vocabulary suddenly becomes usable.

The Japanese passport, incidentally, is consistently ranked among the most powerful in the world for visa-free travel. パスポートを見みせてください (please show your passport) — a sentence you'll hear at every border.

Both kanji have the tree radical 木き — because chairs were made of wood. 椅い specifically means chair (you'll almost never see it outside this word). 子す here is a diminutive suffix, not "child" — it softens the word the way "-let" does in English (booklet, droplet). A little wooden sitting-thing.

Traditional Japanese rooms don't have chairs. You sit on the floor, on a 座すわ布団ふとん (ざぶとん, floor cushion), or in 正ただ座すわ (せいざ, formal kneeling position). Chairs are fundamentally Western furniture that became common in Japan only during modernization. The word 椅子いす itself came from Chinese, but the object it describes was long associated with foreign-style rooms. Today chairs are everywhere, of course — but the older floor-sitting culture persists in tatami rooms, traditional restaurants, and tea ceremony.

椅い子こに座すわる (sit in/on a chair). Note the particle: に, not の上うえに. You sit "at" a chair, not "on top of" it, in Japanese spatial logic.

かわいい is arguably the most culturally loaded adjective in modern Japanese. It means "cute" but it's applied to an astonishing range of things — babies, puppies, fashion, handwriting, behavior, food presentation, elderly people, grown men, inanimate objects. Calling something かわいい is one of the most common positive reactions in Japanese social life. It's not just a description; it's a mode of approval.

The word originally meant something darker. In classical Japanese, かわいい (written 可か哀想 or derived from かわいそう) meant "pitiable" — something so small and helpless it stirred your sympathy. Over centuries, that protective tenderness evolved into affection, and affection into "cute." The vulnerability at the word's core hasn't fully disappeared — かわいい things tend to be small, soft, round, harmless. The aesthetic values the unthreatening.

The kanji 可愛かわいい is rarely written — かわいい in hiragana is standard. When you do see the kanji, 可か means "possible" and 愛あい means "love" — "possible to love," "lovable." Which is, honestly, a pretty good definition of cute.

Dictionaryかわいい
Negativeかわいくない
Pastかわいかった
Past negかわいくなかった
Adverbかわいく
て-formかわいくて

Japanese has two words for grandfather, and the choice depends on whose grandfather you're talking about. おじいさん (with the honorific お〜さん wrapper) is for someone else's grandfather, or for addressing your own grandfather directly. When talking about your own grandfather to others, you'd use the humble 祖父そふ (そふ) instead. This in-group/out-group distinction runs through all Japanese family terms.

おじいさん also means "old man" or "elderly gentleman" in general — you can call any older man おじいさん the way English might use "grandpa" loosely. But be careful: calling someone おじいさん when they don't consider themselves old is a quick way to offend. The boundary between おじさん (middle-aged man) and おじいさん (old man) is one people defend fiercely.

Sound trap: おじいさん (grandfather, long じい) vs. おじさん (uncle/middle-aged man, short じ). That one extra beat of vowel length is the entire difference between "grandpa" and "mister." Stretch the wrong one and you've aged someone thirty years.

切手きって literally means "cut-hand" — which sounds bizarre until you learn the history. In old Japan, 切手きって was a receipt or coupon that you "cut off" (切きる) from a larger document as proof of payment. The postal system adopted the word for its prepaid adhesive stamps, and the original meaning faded. The "hand" (手て) here means document or certificate, not a body part — the same usage as in 手紙かみてがみ (てがみ, letter).

Japan takes stamp collecting seriously. The postal service regularly issues special commemorative 切手きって with artwork, seasonal themes, anime characters, and local landmarks. 切手てきってを貼る (はる, to stick/affix a stamp) — note the verb: 貼はる, the same one used for stickers and wallpaper. You don't "put" a stamp, you "stick" it.

80円えん切手てきってをください (an 80-yen stamp, please) is the sentence you'll use at the post office. The price goes right before 切手きって as a modifier.

Japan has a dedicated vocabulary for temperature that's more precise than English. 涼すずしい fills one very specific slot: the pleasant cool of a breeze on a warm day, the first relief of autumn air, the shade under a tree in summer. It's nice cool — never unpleasant. If the cool becomes uncomfortable, you've crossed into 寒さむい (さむい, cold) territory.

The temperature words form a neat chain: 暑あつい (hot weather) → 涼しい (pleasantly cool) → 寒さむい (cold weather). And for things you touch: 熱あつい (hot to touch) → 冷つめたい (cold to touch). 涼すずしい only works for ambient temperature and air — you'd never describe an ice cube or a cold drink as 涼すずしい.

The kanji 涼りょう has the water radical 氵さんずい on the left — coolness associated with water, evaporation, relief. 涼すずしい風かぜ (a cool breeze) is one of those phrases that practically carries a physical sensation.

Dictionaryすずしい
Negativeすずしくない
Pastすずしかった
Past negすずしくなかった
Adverbすずしく
て-formすずしくて

いくつ is いく (how many) + つ (the native Japanese counter). It asks "how many?" when you're counting generic things with the つ system (一ひとつ、二ふたつ、三みっつ…). りんごはいくつ？ (how many apples?). Simple enough.

The surprise is the second meaning: "how old are you?" おいくつですか？ is a standard (and polite, with the お) way to ask someone's age. Why? Because the old way of counting years used the つ counter — 一ひとつ、二ふたつ… — for ages up to about nine. The practice faded, but the question word stuck. So おいくつですか is literally "how many (years)?" fossilized into a polite age question.

いくつ vs いくら: Both start with いく (how many/much), but いくつ counts discrete things and いくら asks about price or amount. いくつ？ = how many? / how old? いくら？ = how much (money)? Don't mix them up — asking いくら about someone's age sounds like you're pricing them.

From French via English: menu → メニュー. The ニュー part trips up English speakers who want to say "men-oo" — in Japanese it's closer to me-nyu-u, three clean beats with that distinctly Japanese にゅ combination sound.

In a Japanese restaurant, メニューをください gets you the menu. But メニュー extends beyond food — it's used for any list of options: a computer menu, a spa menu of treatments, a set of choices. 今日きょうのメニュー can mean "today's offerings" in any context.

At many Japanese restaurants, especially chains and casual spots, the メニュー comes with photographs of every dish. This isn't considered low-end — it's standard, even expected. The visual menu culture means you can often just point. これをください (this one, please) while pointing at the picture is a perfectly normal way to order.

電でん (lightning/electricity) + 気き (energy/spirit) = electric energy. But in everyday Japanese, 電気でんき usually just means "the light" — as in, the light in the room. 電気でんきをつける (turn on the light). 電気でんきを消けす (turn off the light). When your mom yells about leaving the lights on, she's yelling about 電気でんき.

This double meaning — "electricity" the concept and "the light" the household object — is just something you absorb through context. 電気代でんきだい (でんきだい, electricity bill) is clearly about the utility. 電気でんきがついている (the light is on) is clearly about the room light. Nobody gets confused.

電でん is worth learning well — it anchors a massive family of modern vocabulary: 電話はなでんわ (phone), 電車くるまでんしゃ (train), 電でん池 (battery), 電でん子こ (electronic). All of them are "lightning-something" — phone is lightning-talk, train is lightning-car. The kanji 電でん itself shows rain (雨あめ) over a field with a flash — a thunderstorm captured in ink.

勝かつ is intransitive — a fact that surprises English speakers. In English, you "win a game" (transitive). In Japanese, you win at or against: 試合しあいに勝かつ (win at the match), 相あい手てに勝かつ (win against the opponent). You don't "win" the match as a direct object — the match is the arena where winning happens.

Its partner 負まける (まける, to lose) works the same way: 試合あしあいに負まける. These two are a pair you should learn together. 勝かつ and 負まける always take に — the game, the opponent, the challenge.

The kanji 勝しょう has 力ちから (power) at the bottom right. The victorious one is the one with power. It shows up in compounds like 勝利しょうり (しょうり, victory) and 勝負しょうぶ (しょうぶ, match/contest — literally "win-lose"). That last compound pairs our word perfectly with its opposite.

Particles: 勝かつ takes に — the opponent or contest you win against/at. 彼かれに勝かつ (win against him). The に marks the arena or adversary of victory.

Passive に勝かたれる: に marks who beat you. ライバルに勝かたれた (my rival won — and I'm the one affected). Carries that adversative passive nuance.

Causative を／に勝かたせる: making or letting someone win. を for coercive, に for permissive. 子供こどもに勝かたせる (let the kid win).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryかつに
Negativeかたないに
Politeかちますに
Polite negかちませんに
Pastかったに
Polite pastかちましたに
て-formかってに
Volitionalかとうに
Polite volかちましょうに
Passiveかたれるに
Causativeかたせるを／に
Caus-passかたせられるに
Potentialかてるに
Imperativeかてに
Imp なさいかちなさいに
Cond ～ばかてばに
Cond ～たらかったらに

meaning: to lose (in a match/competition), be defeated
lesson:
負まける is the other half of the 勝かつ/負まける pair. Win and lose. Both intransitive, both take に. 試合しあいに負まけた (lost the match). 彼女おんなかのじょに負まけた (lost to her).

But 負まける has a second life that 勝かつ doesn't: discounts. When haggling at a market or asking for a deal, 負まけてください means "give me a discount" — literally "please lose (some of the price)." The seller "loses" by lowering the price. This usage is casual and only works in certain contexts (flea markets, small shops, some electronics districts), but it's charming and worth knowing.

The kanji 負ふ shows a person (人ひと in its bent form) carrying something on their back (貝, the shell). To bear a burden. Losing is carrying the weight of defeat. The same kanji appears in 負ま担 (ふたん, burden) and 負ま傷 (ふしょう, injury) — all things you carry unwillingly.

Particles: 負まける takes に — the opponent or contest you lose to/at. 相あい手てに負まける (lose to the opponent). に marks the force that defeated you.

Passive に負まけられる: rare, since 負ける is already about receiving an outcome. When used, に marks who lost (affecting you): 味あじ方かたに負まけられると困こまる (it's a problem if our side loses).

Causative を／に負まけさせる: making someone lose. 相あい手てを負まけさせる (make the opponent lose / defeat them).

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 勝つ (かつ)
Dictionaryまけるに
Negativeまけないに
Politeまけますに
Polite negまけませんに
Pastまけたに
Polite pastまけましたに
て-formまけてに
Volitionalまけように
Polite volまけましょうに
Passiveまけられるに
Causativeまけさせるを／に
Caus-passまけさせられるに
Potentialまけられるに
Imperativeまけろに
Imp なさいまけなさいに
Cond ～ばまければに
Cond ～たらまけたらに

建たてる means to build structures — houses, buildings, monuments. Not crafts, not furniture, not sandcastles. This is serious construction. 家いえを建たてる (build a house). ビルを建たてる (erect a building).

たてる confusion: Japanese has multiple verbs pronounced たてる, and they all mean slightly different things. 建たてる = build structures. 立たてる = stand something up, set up (a plan, a sound). 点てる = brew tea (tea ceremony). The kanji sorts them out in writing, but in speech you rely on context. If someone says いえをたてる, it's always 建たてる. If they say けいかくをたてる (make a plan), that's 立たてる.

The kanji 建けん has the radical 廴えんにょう (a long stride) at the bottom — the idea of setting something up with forward motion, raising something that wasn't there before. Its intransitive partner is 建たつ (たつ, to be built): 新あたらしいビルが建たった (a new building was built / went up).

Particles: 建たてる takes を — the structure is your direct object. 家いえを建たてる. You can add に for where: 駅えきの近ちかくに家いえを建たてる (build a house near the station).

Potential が建たてられる: が marks what can be built. この土つち地ちに家いえが建たてられる (a house can be built on this land).

Passive に建たてられる: に marks who built it. 有名なゆうめいな建た築家いえに建てられた (built by a famous architect).

Causative に…を建たてさせる: に marks who you make build, を marks the structure. 業ぎょう者しゃに家いえを建たてさせる (have a contractor build a house).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 建つ (たつ)

Polite negたてませんを
Pastたてたを
Polite pastたてましたを
て-formたててを
Volitionalたてようを

Causativeたてさせるに／を
Caus-passたてさせられるに
Potentialたてられるが
Imperativeたてろを
Imp なさいたてなさいを
Cond ～ばたてればを
Cond ～たらたてたらを

日にっ (day) + 記き (record) = a daily record. A diary. The kanji couldn't be more transparent.

Japan has a long literary tradition of diary writing. The 土つち佐日にち記 (とさにっき), written around 935 AD, is one of the earliest works of Japanese prose — and it's a travel diary. The 枕まくら草くさ子こ (Pillow Book) is essentially a diary. The form has deep cultural prestige that it never quite achieved in the West.

In modern Japan, keeping a 日記にっき is commonly assigned as school homework — students practice writing by recording what happened each day. 日にち記をつける (to keep a diary) uses the verb つける (to attach/keep at). 日にち記を書かく (to write a diary entry) works for individual entries. The verbs are different because one describes the ongoing habit and the other the specific act of writing.

売うり (selling) + 切きれ (cut off, run out) = sold until cut off. Sold out. The 切きれ here is the same one in 切きれる (to run out, be cut) — the supply line has been severed. Nothing left.

You'll see this word plastered on signs, websites, and vending machine buttons. 売うり切きれ — two characters that crush your hopes at a bakery, a concert box office, or a limited-edition product launch. チケットは売うり切きれです (tickets are sold out). もう売うり切きれた (already sold out) uses the past-tense verb form.

Japan's culture of limited-edition runs and seasonal products means you encounter 売うり切きれ constantly. A special flavor at a convenience store, a collaboration product, a particular train bento — they come and go fast. Learning to read this word on a sign saves you the disappointment of asking.

お巡まわりさん breaks down beautifully. 巡めぐり comes from 巡じゅんる (まわる, to patrol/go around). Add the honorific お prefix and the friendly さん suffix, and you get "the honorable one who goes around" — the officer on patrol. It's affectionate, approachable, the word a child would use to flag down a cop.

This is the casual, everyday word for police officer. The formal equivalent is 警察けいさつ官 (けいさつかん), which you'd hear on the news. お巡まわりさん is what you'd say to a neighbor, in a story, or when teaching kids about who to go to if they're lost.

Japanese police officers really do patrol on foot and by bicycle through neighborhoods — the 交番ばんこうばん (こうばん, police box) system puts small stations throughout residential areas, and officers walk regular routes. The word お巡まわりさん reflects this reality: the friendly figure making rounds, not a distant authority behind a desk.

Four kanji, perfectly logical. 目覚めざまし means "eye-awakening" — from 目め (eye) + 覚おぼます (to wake/awaken). 時とき計 means clock (時とき = time, 計けい = measure). An eye-awakening time-measurer. An alarm clock.

The word is long — six syllables, me-za-ma-shi-do-ke-i — which is why Japanese often shortens it to just 目覚めざまし in casual speech. 目め覚おぼましをかける (set the alarm). 目め覚おぼましが鳴った (the alarm went off — literally "rang").

Note how 時とき計 becomes どけい here, not とけい. This is 連れん濁 (rendaku) — the voicing that happens when a word becomes the second element of a compound. The unvoiced と shifts to voiced ど. You've seen this before: 花はな火 (はなび, not はなひ). It's not consistent — some compounds do it, some don't — but 時とき計 always voices in compounds.

From English "receipt" — re-shi-i-to. The "c" disappears (it's silent in English too) and the "p" and "t" each get their own vowel, as katakana demands.

In Japanese shops, the cashier will often ask レシートはよろしいですか — which literally means "is the receipt all right?" but actually means "do you want the receipt?" Saying いいです or 大丈夫だいじょうぶです means you don't need it. This is a daily micro-interaction you'll have hundreds of times in Japan.

レシート vs 領収書: Both mean receipt, but they're different documents. レシート is the register printout — the cash register tape. 領収書りょうしゅうしょ (りょうしゅうしょ) is a formal, handwritten receipt with the store's stamp, used for business expenses and tax purposes. If you need to expense something, you ask for a 領収書りょうしゅうしょ, not a レシート.

From English "tissue" — ti-sshu. The ティ combination is relatively modern in Japanese — older speakers might say チッシュ instead, since ティ didn't exist in traditional katakana. The small ィ marks it as a newer phonetic adaptation.

Pocket tissue packs are a defining feature of Japanese street life. Companies hire people to hand out free ティッシュ at train stations and busy intersections — each pack has an advertisement tucked inside. It's one of the most distinctly Japanese marketing tactics, and it means most people in Japan are carrying tissues at all times. ティッシュをもらう (receive a tissue pack) — a daily occurrence near any major station.

This matters because many public restrooms in Japan, especially older ones, don't provide toilet paper. Having a pocket ティッシュ on you isn't just for sneezing — it's practical infrastructure.

歯は (tooth) + ブラシ (brush, from English) = toothbrush. Half kanji, half katakana — a hybrid word that shows how naturally Japanese absorbs foreign elements. The tooth part is native; the brush part is imported.

ブラシ alone means any kind of brush — hairbrush, paintbrush, scrub brush. Sticking 歯は in front specifies it for teeth. This pattern works broadly: 歯は磨き (はみがき, toothpaste — literally "tooth-polishing"), 歯は医者 (はいしゃ, dentist — literally "tooth-doctor").

At Japanese hotels, 歯はブラシ is typically provided as a disposable amenity, individually wrapped. The quality is usually minimal — a thin handle, basic bristles. Many travelers bring their own. 歯はブラシを持もってきた？ (did you bring a toothbrush?) — a practical travel question.

meaning: to go down, descend, get off (bus, train, etc), land
lesson:
下おりる is downward movement: descending stairs, getting off a train, climbing down a mountain, a plane landing. The kanji 下した (below/down) makes the direction clear. 電車くるまでんしゃを下したりる (get off the train). 山やまを下したりる (descend the mountain).

下りる vs 降りる: These are the same word, おりる, with two possible kanji. 下おりる emphasizes physically going downward — descending stairs, coming down from a height. 降おりる emphasizes getting off or out of a vehicle. In practice, most people write 降おりる for the train/bus sense and 下おりる for literal descent, but they're interchangeable and the kana おりる works for both.

The を particle here is worth noting. 下おりる is intransitive — you're not acting on the train — but を marks the place of departure, the same way it does with 出でる. バスを下したりる = leave the bus by going down and out of it.

Particles: 下おりる takes を to mark the vehicle or place you exit — バスを下したりる. This を isn't a direct object — it marks the departure point. For descending to a place, use に: 一いち階に下したりる (go down to the first floor).

Passive に下したりられる: someone's getting off affects you. 途中なかで下したりられると困こまる (it'd be a problem if they got off midway).

Causative を／に下したりさせる: making or letting someone get off. ここで下したりさせてください (please let me off here).

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 下ろす (おろす)
Dictionaryおりるを
Negativeおりないを
Politeおりますを
Polite negおりませんを
Pastおりたを
Polite pastおりましたを
て-formおりてを
Volitionalおりようを
Polite volおりましょうを
Passiveおりられるに
Causativeおりさせるを／に
Caus-passおりさせられるに
Potentialおりられるを
Imperativeおりろを
Imp なさいおりなさいを
Cond ～ばおりればを
Cond ～たらおりたらを

The kanji 洗せん is water (氵さんずい) + ahead/previous (先せん). Think of it as putting water first — water before everything else, because washing comes before cleanliness. The radical does the heavy lifting: if you see 氵さんずい, water is involved.

洗あらう covers all washing — hands, dishes, clothes, cars, hair. 手てを洗あらう (wash your hands). 車くるまを洗あらう (wash the car). お皿さらを洗あらう (wash the dishes). Unlike English, which might use "do" (do the dishes) or "clean" depending on the object, Japanese keeps it simple: if water and scrubbing are involved, it's 洗あらう.

Compounds worth knowing: 洗濯せんたく (せんたく, laundry), 洗面所せんめんじょ (せんめんじょ, washroom/sink area), 洗あら剤 (せんざい, detergent). All built on the same 洗せん character, all about cleaning with water.

Particles: 洗あらう takes を — what you wash is the direct object. 手てを洗あらう, 食た器を洗あらう. You're acting on the thing with water.

Potential が洗あらえる: が — what can be washed becomes the subject. このセーターは洗濯せんたく機で洗える (this sweater can be washed in a machine).

Passive に洗あらわれる: に marks who washes. Uncommon but valid: 波に洗あらわれた岩 (rocks washed by waves).

Causative に…を洗あらわせる: に marks who you make wash, を marks what. 子供こどもに手てを洗あらわせる (make the kid wash their hands).

Borrowed from "part-time" but trimmed to just the first word: パート. In Japanese usage, this specifically means a part-time job — and more specifically, it usually refers to part-time work done by married women, often in retail, food service, or light manufacturing. There's a social dimension English "part-time" doesn't carry.

パート vs アルバイト: Both mean part-time work, but they're aimed at different people. アルバイト (from German Arbeit), often shortened to バイト, is what students and young people do. パート is what homemakers and older adults do. The work might be identical — behind a register, stocking shelves — but the word signals who you are. パートで働はたらいている (I work part-time) implies a married woman supplementing household income. バイトしている implies a student or young person earning spending money.

There's a legal and economic dimension too. Japan's tax system has an income threshold (the so-called "103万円の壁") that affects whether パート workers owe tax, making the distinction between part-time categories practically significant.
